article.title: Tesla Debuts Third-Generation Optimus at Shanghai Expo, Targets Mass Production by Year-End
article.summary: Tesla showcased its first mass-production-ready humanoid robot at AWE 2026 in Shanghai, with plans to begin manufacturing this summer and sell units to the public by the end of 2027.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Tesla unveiled its third-generation Optimus humanoid robot at the Appliance & Electronics World Expo (AWE 2026) in Shanghai on March 12, marking the company's first public showing of what it describes as a mass-production-ready humanoid machine. The exhibit, which also featured the Cybertruck, signals Tesla's deepening commitment to the Chinese market and its ambition to pivot from automaker to robotics manufacturer.

## What We Know

The Gen 3 Optimus has been redesigned from first principles, according to Tesla, with a focus on achieving human-like manual dexterity. The robot's hands now feature 22 degrees of freedom, up from 11 in the previous generation, driven by a tendon-based actuator system with 25 actuators per forearm and hand, totaling 50 across both arms. Tesla says the robot can learn new skills by observing human behavior, leveraging the same vision-based neural network technology developed for its autonomous driving systems.

Tesla CEO Elon Musk has described the Gen 3 as being in its "final stages" of completion, calling it "by far the most advanced robot in the world," according to [Fortune](https://fortune.com/2026/01/22/elon-musk-agonizingly-slow-cybercab-optimus-robot-rollout-tesla/). Production is expected to begin this summer at the company's Fremont, California facility, where Tesla is converting its former Model S and Model X assembly lines into an Optimus manufacturing hub. The company has set a long-term capacity target of one million units per year at Fremont, with Gigafactory Texas expected to eventually support even larger volumes.

Musk has indicated that public sales could begin by the end of 2027, with a target price between $20,000 and $30,000 per unit. However, he has also cautioned that "the early production rate will be agonizingly slow, but eventually end up being insanely fast," according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2025/10/22/elon-musk-frets-over-controlling-teslas-robot-army-as-car-biz-rebounds/).

The Shanghai showcase is strategically significant. China is home to over 90 percent of global humanoid robot sales, and Chinese manufacturers including Unitree, Agibot, and Leju are already shipping units at scale, according to [Rest of World](https://restofworld.org/2026/china-tesla-robot-race/). Tesla's Giga Shanghai is one of its most productive manufacturing hubs, and the company appears to view the Chinese market as both a proving ground and a major sales opportunity for Optimus.

## What We Don't Know

Tesla has not released detailed technical specifications beyond the hand actuator improvements or disclosed how many Gen 3 units it expects to produce in the initial ramp. The company's track record on robotics timelines has drawn scrutiny: as of mid-2025, Tesla had produced only hundreds of Optimus units despite earlier pledges to build thousands, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2025/10/22/elon-musk-frets-over-controlling-teslas-robot-army-as-car-biz-rebounds/).

It remains unclear how the Gen 3 will perform in real-world factory and household environments compared to Chinese competitors that are already deployed commercially. Lian Jye Su, chief analyst at Omdia, has noted that while Tesla remains a credible competitor, Chinese firms hold significant advantages in manufacturing infrastructure and government-backed industrial policy, according to [Rest of World](https://restofworld.org/2026/china-tesla-robot-race/).

Pricing and commercial availability for markets outside the United States have not been announced, nor has Tesla specified which industrial partners might receive early units beyond its own Gigafactories.
